content.problemStatement:
In C++, `std::lock_guard` is a smart mutex wrapper provided by the C++ Standard Library to manage the locking and unlocking of a mutex. It is part of the threading support library and simplifies the process of using mutexes, making your code more robust and less prone to errors related to manual mutex management.

Here's how to use `std::lock_guard`:

1. We define a mutex named `myMutex`.

2. In the `exclusiveAccess` function, we create a `std::lock_guard` object named `lock` by passing the mutex `myMutex` as a parameter. This `lock_guard` locks the mutex when it is constructed (at the beginning of the scope) and unlocks it automatically when it goes out of scope (at the end of the scope). This ensures that the mutex is always correctly locked and unlocked, even if an exception is thrown.

3. Inside the `exclusiveAccess` function, you can safely access the shared resource because the mutex is automatically unlocked when the `lock_guard` object goes out of scope.

4. In the `main` function, we create two threads that call the `exclusiveAccess` function. The use of `std::lock_guard` ensures that only one thread can access the shared resource at a time.

`std::lock_guard` simplifies mutex management and helps prevent issues like deadlocks and resource leaks by ensuring that the mutex is always unlocked when it is no longer needed.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
